metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Composition 11 develops students as versatile, skilled writers capable of communicating with clarity, purpose, and voice across a range of forms and audiences. Students engage deeply with the writing process — reading to inform their writing, drafting and revising with intention, and building an increasingly sophisticated command of language and craft. The course prepares students for post-secondary writing demands and for effective communication in all areas of life.
metadata.general_description: An advanced study of composition and writing craft, developing versatile writers who can communicate with clarity, voice, and purpose across literary and informational forms through deep engagement with the writing process.

big_ideas.statement: The exploration of text and story deepens our understanding of diverse, complex ideas about identity, others, and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Reading deeply and widely fuels better writing — and the writing process deepens our understanding of our own ideas.

big_ideas.statement: People understand text differently depending on their worldviews and perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Skilled writers are aware of how audience and context shape reception — and make deliberate choices about how to communicate with diverse readers.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, geographically, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: Every piece of writing carries the marks of its context, and skilled writers are conscious of the assumptions and values embedded in their own work.

big_ideas.statement: Language shapes ideas and influences others.
big_ideas.elaboration: Composition students learn to wield language with precision and intentionality, understanding that every choice — from word to structure to form — shapes meaning and impact.

big_ideas.statement: Questioning what we hear, read, and view contributes to our ability to be educated and engaged citizens.
big_ideas.elaboration: Strong writers are also critical readers — they interrogate sources, recognize bias, and bring that awareness to their own writing.

big_ideas.statement: People are empowered by being able to communicate effectively.
big_ideas.elaboration: Effective writing opens doors — in education, careers, advocacy, and relationships. Developing as a writer is an act of empowerment.

big_ideas.statement: Engagement with writing processes can support creativity and enhance clarity of expression.
big_ideas.elaboration: The writing process — with its cycles of drafting, feedback, and revision — is not just a means to an end but a space for discovery, thinking, and growth.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Read for enjoyment and to achieve personal goals; recognize and understand First Peoples perspectives and diversity; understand influence of land/place.
i_can_statements: I can read widely for pleasure, and reflect on how my reading is shaping my writing.
I can engage with First Peoples texts as a writer — noticing craft, voice, and structure.
I can analyze how a writer's relationship to place and land shapes their writing.

competencies.raw: Access information for diverse purposes; evaluate relevance, accuracy, and reliability of texts.
i_can_statements: I can research effectively to inform and deepen my writing.
I can evaluate sources for credibility, relevance, and potential bias.
I can synthesize information from multiple sources into my own original argument or narrative.

competencies.raw: Apply comprehension strategies; recognize how forms, formats, and features shape meaning; think critically and creatively; evaluate literary elements; identify bias, contradictions, distortions, omissions.
i_can_statements: I can read mentor texts closely to identify specific craft techniques I can adapt in my own writing.
I can identify and analyze how form, structure, and literary devices create specific effects.
I can recognize bias, distortion, or omission in source material and consider their implications for my writing.
I can ask critical questions of my own writing: What am I assuming? What am I leaving out?

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Respectfully exchange ideas from diverse perspectives to build shared understandings and extend thinking.
i_can_statements: I can participate constructively in writing workshops, giving and receiving specific feedback.
I can engage respectfully with ideas and writing from diverse cultural perspectives.
I can use collaborative discussion to refine and extend my own thinking and writing.

competencies.raw: Respond to text personally, creatively, and critically; demonstrate speaking and listening skills across contexts.
i_can_statements: I can write varied responses to texts — personal, analytical, and creative — with appropriate depth.
I can present my writing effectively for different audiences and contexts.
I can listen critically to feedback and use it to improve my writing.

competencies.raw: Use writing and design processes to plan, develop, and create texts; express and support opinions with evidence; reflect on and refine texts.
i_can_statements: I can move through a rigorous writing process: generating, drafting, workshopping, revising, editing, and finishing.
I can write a clear, well-supported argument with a strong thesis and specific evidence.
I can revise my writing at multiple levels — ideas, structure, paragraph, sentence, and word.

competencies.raw: Use conventions proficiently; use citations; transform ideas to create original texts; experiment with genres and styles.
i_can_statements: I can apply grammar, punctuation, and spelling conventions accurately and contextually.
I can cite all sources properly and explain why intellectual property acknowledgment matters.
I can experiment with genre, voice, and form to find what best serves my purpose.
I can write original texts in multiple modes: analytical, narrative, descriptive, persuasive.

areas.name: Text Forms and Genres
areas.raw: Text forms include narrative, journal, procedural, expository, explanatory, news article, e-mail, blog, advertisements, poetry, novel, and letter. Genres include adventure, fable, fairy tale, fantasy, folklore, historical, horror, legend, mystery, mythology, picture book, science fiction, biography, essay, journalism, manual, memoir, personal narrative, and speech.
topics: Academic writing: analytical essay, argumentative essay, research paper
Personal writing: journal, personal essay, memoir, letter
Literary writing: short story, poem, creative non-fiction
Functional writing: email, report, procedural text
Journalistic writing: feature article, news writing, opinion
Multimodal writing: blog, website, infographic, multimedia essay

areas.name: Text Features and Structures
areas.raw: Typography, font style, guide words, titles, diagrams, captions, labels, maps, charts, illustrations, tables, photographs, sidebars; narrative structures in First Peoples texts; protocols.
topics: Essay structure: introduction, body paragraphs, conclusion
Paragraph structure: topic sentence, evidence, commentary, transition
Narrative structure in creative writing: rising action, climax, denouement
Visual and design elements in multimodal writing
First Peoples narrative structures and protocols for using oral texts

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading strategies; oral language strategies; metacognitive strategies; writing processes (determining audience and purpose, generating ideas, free-writing, making notes, drafting, revising, editing).
topics: Reading to write: annotating mentor texts for craft
Prewriting: brainstorming, mind-mapping, freewriting, outlining
Drafting: prioritizing flow over perfection
Peer and teacher workshop: giving and receiving specific feedback
Revising: global revision (ideas, structure) before local revision (sentences, words)
Editing: grammar, mechanics, citation
Writer's notebook: a tool for observation and idea development

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Elements of style (diction, vocabulary, sentence structure, tone); usage; conventions; citation techniques; literary elements and devices.
topics: Diction: precision, connotation, tone
Sentence variety: length, structure, rhythm for effect
Transitions: logical flow within and between paragraphs
Voice: developing an authentic and confident writing voice
Thesis statements: writing clear, debatable, specific central claims
Evidence and commentary: integrating quotations and explaining their significance
Citation: MLA and APA formats for academic writing
Rhetorical appeals: using ethos, pathos, and logos in persuasive writing
